Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

end of world model.

Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 1: you have used 35 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing RIGHT twice, something very different happened. Looking at the image, I now see:
1. The left frame now has a white square inside it (instead of the green square)
2. There's a green square in the middle area (at the original position)
3. There's another green square to the left of the purple bar
Wait, the image shows TWO green squares now! One in the left frame area and one in the middle. Let me check the segmentation.
Actually, looking more carefully at the image:
- Left frame: contains a white square (not a green square)
- Middle: contains a green square with a white dot
- There's also a green square to the left of the purple bar
Wait, I see two green squares in the middle area. Let me check the segmentation to understand what happened.
Actually, looking at the image again:
- Left frame: contains a white square (the frame is still there, but the green square is gone)
- Middle: contains a green square with a white dot (this is the right green square)
- There's a small green square to the left of the purple bar (this might be the left green square that moved out of the frame)
So pressing RIGHT moved the left green square out of the frame! The left green square was at (34, 13), and pressing RIGHT moved it to the right, out of the frame.
Wait, but I pressed RIGHT twice, and the output said the green squares were at (31, 22) with dot=c and (34, 13) with dot=W. But now the image shows a different configuration. Let me check the current segmentation.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after RIGHT, RIGHT
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}, center={center}, children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

So RIGHT/LEFT moves the left green square when it's in the left frame, and moves the right green square when it's in the middle.

But the level isn't complete. So maybe the goal is not just to have white squares in the frames.

Actually, looking at the image, the left green square is now visible in the middle area, to the left of the purple bar. The right green square is also in the middle area.

Wait, but the purple bar is at columns 35-40 approximately. The left green square is at (34, 19), which is far to the left of the purple bar. So I would need to move it RIGHT many times to reach the purple bar.
Actually, let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is to have the green squares in specific positions with specific dot colors. The human solution is 28 actions, which is a lot. So the solution probably involves moving the green squares around a lot.
Let me try a different approach: what if the goal is to have the left green square in the left frame with a specific dot color, and the right green square in the right frame with a specific dot color?

- Right frame: contains white square (not green square)
- Left green square: at (34, 19) with W dot
- Right green square: at (31, 22) with c dot
To put the left green square back in the left frame, I need to move it LEFT 6 cells (2 LEFT actions).
To put the right green square in the right frame, I need to move it RIGHT past the purple bar. But the purple bar blocks movement to the right.
Wait, maybe the green squares can move UP or DOWN to bypass the purple bar? Let me check if the green squares can move vertically.
